title: The World's Religions: Our Great Wisdom Traditions
excerpt: A sympathetic, insider's introduction to the world's major religious traditions — Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism, Taoism, Islam, Judaism, Christianity, and primal religions — that has sold over three million copies and shaped how generations understand faith.
keyIdeas: Religion must be understood 'from the inside' — by its highest aspirations, not its institutional failures
The world's major religions are distinct paths up the same mountain of transcendent Reality
Each tradition emphasizes a different human faculty: love (Christianity), law (Judaism), submission (Islam), harmony (Taoism), order (Confucianism), discernment (Buddhism), and realization (Hinduism)
Primal religions preserve an immediate, symbolic orientation to the sacred that literate religions have largely lost
keyTakeaways: Hinduism offers four yogic paths — knowledge, love, work, and psychophysical exercises — suited to different temperaments
Buddhism diagnoses suffering as rooted in craving and prescribes the Eightfold Path as its remedy
Confucianism is primarily an ethical system focused on social harmony through right relationships
Taoism complements Confucianism by emphasizing spontaneous alignment with the natural order (wu-wei)
Islam means 'surrender'; its Five Pillars structure a life of submission to the one God (Allah)
Judaism centers on covenant, community memory, and the call to justice in history
Christianity pivots on the person of Jesus Christ — his teachings, his crucifixion, and the faith that he rose again
Primal religions embed the sacred in specific places, seasons, and oral traditions
whoShouldRead: College students taking their first course in world religions
General readers seeking a respectful, accessible introduction to faiths other than their own
Religious practitioners who want to understand their tradition's place in the broader human story
Anyone puzzled by why religion remains powerful in a scientific age
whoShouldSkip: Scholars seeking critical, deconstructive accounts of religious traditions
Readers wanting detailed historical or sociological analysis of institutional religion
Those who prefer a polemical atheist or fundamentalist apologetic approach
